LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD contains only static variable assignments and no top-level command substitutions or function calls that would execute code during sourcing. The `source` array uses standard HTTPS URLs from the official GitHub releases, with fixed checksums (not SKIP) for the two zip files. Auxiliary source files (`.png`, `.desktop`) are local to the PKGBUILD directory, as is typical. There is no `pkgver()` function. The `package()` function is defined but is not executed by `--nobuild`/`--noprepare`. No dangerous commands (eval, curl, base64 decode, etc.) appear in the global scope or `pkgver()`. Fetching sources and sourcing the PKGBUILD will not trigger any malicious behavior.
</details>
<summary>No malicious code executes during source fetch or PKGBUILD sourcing.</summary>
</security_assessment>

The package() function installs standard files (icon, desktop entry, binary bundle) into the expected locations. The permission normalization code that detects ELF files by magic bytes is a creative but harmless approach — it only sets execute bits on actual binaries, no data exfiltration or code injection. </details>

</details>

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/v2rayn-bin.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-dxfrptrj...

Analyzing v2rayn-bin AUR package (working in /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-dxfrptrj) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines sources from the project's official GitHub repository (`https://github.com/2dust/v2rayN`) using predictable release URLs based on `pkgver`. The `source` array includes static `.zip` binaries for x86_64 and arm64, as well as local source files (`v2rayN.png`, `v2rayN-bin.desktop`) that are likely bundled in the AUR package. Checksums are provided for all sources, and while the binary archives are versioned and pinned, there are no signs of dynamic or unverified fetching during `source` resolution.

During `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare`, the only executed code is at the global scope and `pkgver()`, but `pkgver` is hard-coded and does not involve command substitution or network calls. There are no top-level commands, `curl`/`wget` invocations, `eval`, or obfuscated logic that could exfiltrate data or execute payloads while sourcing the PKGBUILD. The source fetches are standard HTTPS downloads from the declared upstream and do not use mutable `git+` protocols or untrusted mirrors.

The `package()` function contains file operations and permission changes, but it is not executed under `--noprepare` or `--nobuild`, so it is out of scope for this narrow gate. Nothing in the parsed PKGBUILD during source acquisition suggests malicious side effects during download or sourcing.
</details>
<summary>
Safe: no remote code execution on source fetch
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Safe: no remote code execution on source fetch[/]

[0/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD, v2rayN-bin.desktop...
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD fetches pre-built binaries from the official GitHub repository of the v2rayN project, matching the declared upstream URL. The source arrays for x86_64 and arm64 reference release binaries (v2rayN-linux-64.zip and v2rayN-linux-arm64.zip) with fixed version tags and pinned SHA-256 checksums, ensuring integrity. No dynamic or untrusted downloads occur during build or package phases. The additional source files (v2rayN.png and v2rayN-bin.desktop) are local and checksummed.

The package() function installs desktop integration files and extracts the binary bundle into /opt/v2rayn-bin. File permissions are sanitized: directories are set to 755, regular files to 644, and only files with the ELF magic bytes (7f454c46) are made executable—this is a safe, deterministic method to mark binaries without relying on original permissions or unsafe commands like eval or base64. There is no use of curl|bash, obfuscated code, runtime fetches, or suspicious system modifications.

All actions are consistent with standard AUR packaging practices for redistributing pre-built binaries. The maintainer does not strip binaries (justified due to .NET AOT requirements) and avoids dangerous operations. No evidence of data exfiltration, remote code execution, or supply-chain compromise is present.
</details>
<summary>
No malicious behavior; standard safe packaging
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing v2rayN-bin.desktop...
+ Reviewed PKGBUILD. Status: SAFE -- No malicious behavior; standard safe packaging
LLM auditresponse for v2rayN-bin.desktop:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided `.desktop` file is a standard desktop entry configuration used to integrate the v2rayN application into the desktop environment's application menu and launch system.

It specifies metadata such as the application name, icon, categories, and execution command, all of which are typical for desktop entries. The `Exec` field points to `/opt/v2rayn-bin/v2rayN`, which is a standard path for a binary installed under a dedicated directory, and `Terminal=false` correctly indicates a GUI application. There are no network operations, shell commands, obfuscation attempts, or unexpected behaviors in this file.

The file does not contain any scripts, executable logic, or data exfiltration mechanisms. It is declarative and consistent with normal desktop integration practices for Arch Linux applications.
</details>
<summary>
Standard desktop entry, no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for geoip-google.srs:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `geoip-google.srs` appears to be a binary or compiled data file, likely used for geolocation lookups, possibly by the V2RayN application. The truncated content shows no signs of executable code, scripts, or commands such as `curl`, `wget`, `eval`, `base64`, or `exec`. There is no evidence of network communication, obfuscation, or malicious payloads.

The data appears to be structured in a proprietary format (possibly SRS—Static Route Storage or similar), including human-readable fragments like "iTuTU" and "ZwrhbQa", which may be artifacts of encoding or compression. No suspicious domains, IP addresses, or command invocations are present.

Given that the file is distributed as part of the `v2rayn-bin` package—a precompiled binary package—and considering common practices for such packages, this file is consistent with legitimate application data (e.g., GeoIP or routing rules). There are no indicators of tampering, backdoors, or supply-chain compromise.
</details>
<summary>
Binary data file; no malicious indicators found.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing src/v2rayN-linux-64/bin/geosite.dat...
+ Reviewed src/v2rayN-linux-64/bin/srss/geoip-google.srs. Status: SAFE -- Binary data file; no malicious indicators found.
LLM auditresponse for geosite.dat:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `geosite.dat` is a binary data file used for domain categorization, likely by a networking or proxy application such as V2Ray or Xray. Its contents consist of structured records mapping domain names and patterns to categories (e.g., CATEGORY-CRYPTOCURRENCY, CATEGORY-SPEEDTEST), which is standard functionality for geolocation or domain-based routing tools.

No executable code, scripts, or suspicious commands (e.g., `curl`, `wget`, `eval`, `base64`) are present. The entries are domain names, some with regex patterns, grouped under descriptive categories. Many domains belong to legitimate services (e.g., Apple, Microsoft, Cloudflare) and are listed with region-specific overrides (e.g., `linkcn` indicating China-specific rules). These are typical for routing behavior in network tools.

Despite the large size and inclusion of advertising or tracking-related domains (e.g., under `ads` tags), there is no evidence of malicious payload delivery, data exfiltration, or unauthorized behavior. The file’s structure and content align with known use cases in open-source proxy software.
</details>
<summary>
Binary domain list file, no malicious code detected
</summary>
</security_assessment>
